docs.title: Building the next generation of AI-enabled finance professionals
docs.text:
Finance is evolving.

Artificial Intelligence is changing how professionals research companies, analyse financial statements, build models, automate workflows, and make decisions.

However, technology alone does not create better finance professionals.

The future belongs to those who combine:

Financial Expertise + Business Judgment + AI Capability

AI Finance Lab is an open learning platform designed to help students, analysts, and finance professionals develop these skills.

docs.title: Building the bridge between Finance and Artificial Intelligence
docs.text:
Finance has always evolved alongside technology.

The calculator changed financial analysis.

The spreadsheet transformed financial modelling.

Enterprise systems reshaped reporting.

Artificial Intelligence represents the next major transformation.

AI Finance Lab was created to explore a simple question:

How can finance professionals use AI to become better analysts, decision-makers, and business partners?

docs.title: The Mission
docs.text:
AI Finance Lab aims to make practical AI-enabled finance education accessible to students, analysts, and professionals around the world.

The goal is not to replace financial expertise with technology.

The goal is to combine:

Finance knowledge + Business judgment + AI capability

to create the next generation of finance professionals.

docs.text: Today, there are two rapidly growing worlds:

A technology revolutionizing: - Data analysis - Automation - Research - Productivity - Decision support

However, the intersection between these fields remains underdeveloped.

AI Finance Lab exists to bridge that gap.

docs.title: Combining Financial Expertise with Artificial Intelligence
docs.text:
Financial modelling has traditionally required significant time spent on:

  • Data collection
  • Spreadsheet construction
  • Formula writing
  • Error checking
  • Documentation
  • Scenario analysis

Artificial Intelligence is changing how finance professionals approach these tasks.

However, AI does not replace financial modelling expertise.

The strongest professionals will combine:

Financial Modelling Skills + AI Capability + Professional Judgement

docs.title: What Is AI-Assisted Financial Modelling?
docs.text:
AI-assisted financial modelling refers to using artificial intelligence tools to improve the process of building, reviewing, and communicating financial models.

AI can support:

  • Research
  • Data extraction
  • Formula creation
  • Model documentation
  • Error identification
  • Scenario analysis
  • Financial analysis

The objective is not to automate the entire modelling process.

The objective is to improve productivity while maintaining analytical quality.

docs.title: Why AI Matters in Financial Modelling
docs.text:
Traditional modelling workflows involve many repetitive tasks.

Examples:

  • Reading annual reports
  • Extracting historical financial data
  • Formatting spreadsheets
  • Writing repetitive formulas
  • Creating documentation

AI can accelerate these activities, allowing finance professionals to spend more time on higher-value activities:

  • Understanding the business
  • Evaluating assumptions
  • Interpreting results
  • Making recommendations

docs.title: The AI-Enabled Modelling Workflow
docs.text:
The future modelling workflow looks like:

Business Understanding
        ↓
Data Collection
        ↓
AI-Assisted Research
        ↓
Model Construction
        ↓
AI Review & Quality Checks
        ↓
Professional Validation
        ↓
Decision Making

AI supports every stage, but human judgement remains central.

docs.title: 1. AI-Assisted Research
docs.text:
Before building a model, analysts need to understand the company.

AI can help analyse:

  • Annual reports
  • Investor presentations
  • Earnings transcripts
  • Industry reports

Example tasks:

  • Summarise business segments
  • Identify revenue drivers
  • Extract historical financial data
  • Highlight management commentary

However:

Always verify extracted information against original sources.

docs.title: 2. AI-Assisted Historical Data Collection
docs.text:
Building models often starts with collecting historical financial information.

AI can assist with:

  • Extracting financial statement line items
  • Organising historical data
  • Identifying trends
  • Creating structured datasets

Example:

An analyst may provide an annual report and ask:

Extract five years of revenue, EBITDA, depreciation, capital expenditure, and working capital information into a structured table.

The output should then be reviewed before entering the model.

docs.title: 3. AI-Assisted Formula Development
docs.text:
AI can help finance professionals:

  • Explain formulas
  • Suggest spreadsheet functions
  • Troubleshoot errors
  • Improve formula readability

Example:

A user may ask:

Explain how to calculate working capital changes in a three-statement model.

AI can provide:

  • Formula explanation
  • Logic walkthrough
  • Common modelling approaches

However, the modeller must understand why the formula works.

docs.title: 4. AI-Assisted Model Review
docs.text:
One of the highest-value applications is model auditing.

AI can help identify:

  • Broken formulas
  • Inconsistent calculations
  • Hardcoded assumptions
  • Missing links
  • Potential errors

Example review questions:

  • Are the financial statements linked correctly?
  • Why does the balance sheet not balance?
  • Are assumptions consistent?
  • Are formulas following modelling best practices?

docs.title: 5. AI-Assisted Scenario Analysis
docs.text:
Financial models are designed to test different outcomes.

AI can help generate scenarios:

docs.title: Base Case
docs.text: Most likely assumptions.

docs.title: Upside Case
docs.text: Improved business performance.

docs.title: Downside Case
docs.text:
Potential risks.

Examples:

  • Lower revenue growth
  • Margin compression
  • Higher interest rates
  • Increased costs

AI can help explore possibilities faster.

docs.title: 6. AI-Assisted Model Documentation
docs.text:
Documentation is an important but often neglected part of modelling.

AI can help create:

  • Assumption explanations
  • Formula descriptions
  • Model guides
  • Investment summaries

Good documentation improves:

  • Review efficiency
  • Knowledge transfer
  • Model usability

docs.title: Example: AI-Assisted DCF Workflow
docs.text:
A traditional DCF process:

  1. Read annual report
  2. Extract historical financials
  3. Forecast revenue
  4. Forecast margins
  5. Calculate free cash flow
  6. Determine discount rate
  7. Calculate valuation

AI can assist with:

  1. Summarising annual reports
  2. Extracting historical data
  3. Generating initial forecast structures
  4. Explaining valuation concepts
  5. Reviewing calculations

The analyst remains responsible for:

  • Forecast assumptions
  • Business judgement
  • Valuation conclusions

docs.title: What AI Cannot Replace
docs.text: AI cannot independently determine:

docs.title: Business Quality
docs.text: Is the company strategically strong?

docs.title: Forecast Reasonableness
docs.text: Are growth assumptions realistic?

docs.title: Competitive Advantage
docs.text: Does the company have sustainable differentiation?

docs.title: Investment Decision
docs.text: Should someone buy, sell, or invest?

docs.title: 1. Understand Before Automating
docs.text: Never automate a process you do not understand.

docs.title: 2. Verify AI Outputs
docs.text:
Always check:

  • Sources
  • Numbers
  • Formulas
  • Logic

docs.title: 3. Use AI as an Assistant
docs.text: The modeller remains responsible for the final output.

docs.title: 4. Maintain Professional Standards
docs.text:
Follow traditional modelling principles:

  • Clear structure
  • Consistent formatting
  • Audit checks
  • Logical assumptions

docs.title: Understanding Artificial Intelligence for Finance Professionals
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming how finance professionals analyse businesses, build financial models, conduct research, and make decisions.

Rather than viewing AI as a replacement for finance expertise, AI Finance Lab views AI as a powerful collaborator.

The professionals who thrive in the coming decade will combine:

  • Strong finance fundamentals
  • Business judgement
  • AI fluency

docs.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence refers to computer systems designed to perform tasks that traditionally require human intelligence.

Examples include:

  • Understanding language
  • Recognising patterns
  • Generating text
  • Writing code
  • Summarising documents
  • Answering questions

Modern AI systems are particularly effective at processing large amounts of information quickly.

docs.title: What is Generative AI?
docs.text:
Generative AI creates new content based on patterns learned from existing data.

In the financial context, it can generate:

  • Reports
  • Summaries
  • Financial analysis
  • Code
  • Presentations
  • Spreadsheets
  • Charts
  • Explanations

For finance professionals, Generative AI acts like a highly knowledgeable research assistant—not a replacement for professional judgement.

docs.title: How Finance Professionals Use AI
docs.text: AI is already helping professionals:

docs.text:
• Summarise annual reports
  • Compare competitors
  • Review earnings calls

docs.text:

docs.text:
• Suggest Excel formulas
  • Build simple calculations
  • Explain modelling logic
  • Generate documentation

docs.title: Productivity
docs.text:
• Draft emails
  • Create presentations
  • Summarise meetings
  • Generate reports

docs.title: What AI Does Well
docs.text:
AI excels at:

  • Processing information quickly
  • Explaining concepts
  • Generating first drafts
  • Finding patterns
  • Organising information
  • Automating repetitive tasks

docs.title: What AI Does Poorly
docs.text:
AI may:

  • Hallucinate facts
  • Misinterpret context
  • Produce incorrect calculations
  • Use outdated information
  • Sound confident while being wrong

Understanding these limitations is essential for every finance professional.

docs.title: Human Judgement Still Matters
docs.text:
Finance requires:

  • Critical thinking
  • Commercial judgement
  • Ethical decision-making
  • Risk assessment
  • Context

These remain human responsibilities.

docs.title: The AI Finance Mindset
docs.text:
Think of AI as:

An intelligent junior analyst that works incredibly fast—but whose work must always be reviewed.

docs.title: Next Module
docs.text: ➡ Prompt Engineering

docs.title: Building Trust Through Verification
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence has the ability to transform how finance professionals work.

It can summarise reports, analyse data, generate financial insights, and automate repetitive tasks.

However, professional finance requires more than speed.

It requires accuracy, judgement, and accountability.

AI-generated outputs should never be accepted without review.

The ability to evaluate, challenge, and verify AI outputs is a critical skill for the AI-enabled finance professional.

docs.title: Why Auditing Matters
docs.text:
Finance professionals make decisions involving:

  • Investments
  • Capital allocation
  • Business strategy
  • Risk management
  • Financial reporting

Errors in these areas can have significant consequences.

A financial analyst would never accept an Excel model without checking:

  • formulas,
  • assumptions,
  • calculations,
  • logic.

The same principle applies to AI.

AI outputs require professional review.

docs.title: The AI Audit Mindset
docs.text:
The correct mindset is:

Trust the process, not the output.

AI should be treated like a highly capable junior analyst.

A junior analyst may:

  • work quickly,
  • identify useful information,
  • create a first draft,

but their work still requires review.

AI is no different.

docs.title: 1. Hallucinations
docs.text:
AI models may generate information that appears credible but is incorrect.

Examples:

  • Invented facts
  • Incorrect company information
  • Fake citations
  • Incorrect financial figures

Always verify important information against reliable sources.

docs.title: 2. Incorrect Calculations
docs.text:
AI can explain financial concepts but may occasionally make mathematical errors.

Examples:

  • Incorrect valuation calculations
  • Wrong percentage changes
  • Incorrect financial ratios

Important calculations should be independently verified.

docs.title: 3. Missing Context
docs.text:
AI may provide technically correct information but fail to understand the broader business context.

Example:

A declining margin may indicate:

  • increased costs,
  • strategic investment,
  • pricing pressure,
  • temporary disruption.

Numbers require interpretation.

docs.title: 4. Outdated Information
docs.text:
AI models may not always have access to the latest information.

Always verify:

  • financial statements,
  • market data,
  • regulatory changes,
  • company announcements.

docs.title: The AI Audit Framework
docs.text: A professional AI review process can follow five steps.

docs.title: Step 1: Verify the Source
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Where did this information come from?
  • Is the source reliable?
  • Can the claim be independently confirmed?

For finance analysis, prioritise:

  • Annual reports
  • Regulatory filings
  • Investor presentations
  • Company disclosures

docs.title: Step 2: Check the Logic
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Does the conclusion make sense?
  • Are assumptions reasonable?
  • Does the reasoning follow?

Example:

AI says:

"Revenue growth increased because profitability improved."

Question:

"Is revenue growth actually related to profitability?"

The conclusion may not follow.

docs.title: Step 3: Validate the Numbers
docs.text:
Check:

  • Calculations
  • Percentages
  • Financial ratios
  • Valuation outputs

Example:

If AI calculates a revenue growth rate:

Revenue Growth =
(Current Year Revenue - Prior Year Revenue)
/
Prior Year Revenue

Verify the calculation independently.

docs.title: Step 4: Challenge Assumptions
docs.text:
Every financial analysis depends on assumptions.

Examples:

  • Revenue growth
  • Margin expansion
  • Discount rate
  • Terminal growth rate

Ask:

  • Why is this assumption reasonable?
  • What evidence supports it?
  • What happens if the assumption changes?

docs.title: Step 5: Apply Professional Judgement
docs.text:
The final responsibility belongs to the finance professional.

AI can provide:

  • Information
  • Analysis
  • Suggestions

The professional provides:

  • Context
  • Experience
  • Decision-making

docs.title: AI Auditing in Financial Modelling
docs.text:
Financial models provide a useful example.

AI can help:

  • Generate formulas
  • Explain spreadsheet logic
  • Review model structure
  • Identify possible errors

However, a finance professional must still check:

docs.title: Formula Accuracy
docs.text: Does the formula calculate what it should?

docs.title: Model Integrity
docs.text: Do the statements balance?

docs.title: Assumption Quality
docs.text: Are forecasts realistic?

docs.title: Business Logic
docs.text: Does the model reflect how the company actually operates?

docs.title: Example: AI-Assisted Company Analysis
docs.text:
Imagine asking AI:

"Analyse Breville Group's financial performance."

AI may provide:

  • Revenue trends
  • Margin analysis
  • Strategic observations

A finance professional should then ask:

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Are these figures from the annual report?

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Why did performance change?

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text: What does this mean for future performance?

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: What action should an investor or manager take?

docs.title: AI Auditing Checklist
docs.text:
Before using AI output in professional work:

Question Check Is the information accurate? Verify sources Are calculations correct? Recalculate Are assumptions reasonable? Challenge inputs Does the logic make sense? Review reasoning Would I present this to a senior stakeholder? Apply judgement

docs.title: AI + Finance Professional Workflow
docs.text:
The future workflow is not:

Question → AI Answer → Decision

It is:

Question
   ↓
AI Assistance
   ↓
Professional Review
   ↓
Validation
   ↓
Decision

The value comes from combining AI speed with human judgement.

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this module, you should understand:

  • Why AI outputs require verification
  • Common AI risks in finance
  • How to audit AI-generated analysis
  • How to combine AI efficiency with professional judgement

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Using your preferred AI assistant, generate an AI summary of the Breville FY2025 Annual Report.

Then audit the output by checking:

  • Are all financial figures correct?
  • Are revenue growth rates accurate?
  • Has AI invented information that is not in the report?
  • Are management comments represented fairly?
  • Are important risks omitted?

Record every error you identify.

Goal: Develop the habit of validating AI outputs before relying on them in professional finance work.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
You are now ready to apply AI to practical finance workflows:

  • Financial statement analysis
  • Annual report analysis
  • Financial modelling
  • Valuation
  • Investment research

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence is only as effective as the instructions it receives.

Just as a financial model is only as reliable as its assumptions, AI outputs are only as useful as the prompts that guide them.

Learning how to communicate effectively with AI is one of the highest-leverage skills a modern finance professional can develop.

This lesson introduces the principles of effective prompting and demonstrates how they can be applied across financial analysis, modelling, valuation, and investment research.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
After completing this lesson, you should be able to:

  • Understand what prompts are and why they matter.
  • Write structured prompts for finance tasks.
  • Improve the quality and consistency of AI-generated outputs.
  • Reduce hallucinations and factual errors.
  • Apply prompting techniques to real finance workflows.

docs.title: What is a Prompt?
docs.text:
A prompt is simply an instruction given to an AI model.

Think of AI as a highly capable junior analyst.

If your instructions are vague, incomplete, or ambiguous, the output is likely to be equally vague.

If your instructions are clear, specific, and well-structured, the output becomes significantly more useful.

Good prompting is not about using complicated words.

It is about communicating clearly.

docs.title: Why Prompting Matters in Finance
docs.text:
Finance professionals work with information that must be:

  • Accurate
  • Structured
  • Evidence-based
  • Reproducible

Unlike creative writing, finance has little tolerance for ambiguity.

Poor prompts often result in:

  • Missing assumptions
  • Incorrect calculations
  • Unsupported conclusions
  • Generic responses
  • Hallucinated information

Well-designed prompts improve both the quality and reliability of AI-generated work.

docs.title: The Anatomy of an Effective Prompt
docs.text: A high-quality prompt typically contains five components.

docs.title: 1. Role
docs.text:
Tell the AI who it should be.

Example:

Act as an Equity Research Analyst.

or

Act as an Investment Banking Associate.

docs.title: 2. Context
docs.text:
Provide the necessary background.

Example:

You are analysing Breville Group's FY2025 Annual Report to assess the company's financial performance and valuation.

Context reduces ambiguity and helps the AI produce more relevant responses.

docs.title: 3. Task
docs.text:
Describe exactly what you want the AI to do.

Instead of:

Analyse this company.

Try:

Identify the primary revenue drivers, key risks, and margin trends from the annual report.

Specific tasks produce better outputs.

docs.title: 4. Constraints
docs.text:
Define the boundaries.

Examples:

  • Use only information contained in the annual report.
  • Do not make assumptions that are not explicitly supported.
  • Present findings as bullet points.
  • Limit the response to 500 words.

Constraints improve consistency and reduce hallucinations.

docs.title: 5. Output Format
docs.text:
Specify how the response should be structured.

For example:

  • Executive Summary
  • Bullet Points
  • Markdown Table
  • Excel Formula
  • JSON
  • Investment Memo

Well-defined output formats save time and improve readability.

docs.title: A Simple Prompt Framework
docs.text:
A useful framework for finance professionals is:

Role

↓

Context

↓

Task

↓

Constraints

↓

Output Format

Example:

Act as an Equity Research Analyst.

You are reviewing Breville Group's FY2025 Annual Report.

Identify the three most important business risks discussed by management.

Use only information contained within the report.

Present the results as a table with Risk, Evidence and Potential Financial Impact.

docs.title: Example: Poor Prompt
docs.text:
Summarise Breville.

Possible outcome:

  • Generic information
  • Limited detail
  • Inconsistent structure
  • Unsupported assumptions

docs.title: Example: Improved Prompt
docs.text:
Act as a senior equity research analyst.

Review Breville Group's FY2025 Annual Report.

Summarise the company's business model, revenue drivers, operating margins, geographic exposure, competitive advantages and key investment risks.

Use only evidence from the annual report.

Present the response as an executive summary suitable for an investment committee.

Notice how the improved prompt clearly defines:

  • Role
  • Context
  • Task
  • Constraints
  • Output format

docs.title: Prompting is an Iterative Process
docs.text:
Rarely does the first prompt produce the perfect result.

Professional prompting follows an iterative workflow.

Prompt

↓

Review Output

↓

Identify Gaps

↓

Refine Prompt

↓

Repeat

Each iteration improves the quality of the analysis.

docs.title: Asking Multiple Questions at Once
docs.text:
Avoid:

Analyse Breville, build a DCF, estimate WACC, explain the industry and compare competitors.

Instead, break complex tasks into smaller steps.

docs.title: Being Too Vague
docs.text:
Avoid:

Explain DCF.

Instead:

Explain how Discounted Cash Flow valuation is used to estimate enterprise value, including Free Cash Flow, WACC and Terminal Value.

docs.title: Assuming AI Knows Your Context
docs.text:
Always provide:

  • Company
  • Reporting period
  • Objective
  • Audience

Context matters.

docs.title: Accepting the First Response
docs.text:
Always review:

  • Assumptions
  • Calculations
  • Sources
  • Logic
  • Consistency

AI should support professional judgement—not replace it.

docs.title: Prompting in the AI Finance Lab
docs.text:
Throughout this lab, prompting will be used to:

  • Analyse annual reports
  • Extract financial information
  • Generate modelling assumptions
  • Build financial models
  • Review Excel formulas
  • Audit financial models
  • Draft investment memos
  • Prepare management presentations

Prompting is not a standalone skill.

It is embedded into every stage of the finance workflow.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Prompting is the process of communicating effectively with AI.
  • Clear prompts produce better outputs.
  • Structured prompts reduce ambiguity.
  • Prompting is iterative.
  • Professional judgement remains essential.
  • AI should augment—not replace—the finance professional.

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Using your preferred AI assistant, write prompts that answer the following questions:

  • What does Breville do?
  • How does the company generate revenue?
  • What are the company's primary brands and products?
  • Which geographic markets contribute most to revenue?
  • What competitive advantages does management highlight?
  • What risks are discussed in the report?

Compare the AI-generated responses with the annual report and refine your prompts until the answers are accurate, well-structured, and supported by evidence.

Goal: Learn how better prompts produce better financial analysis.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Continue to Learn Auditing, where you will learn how to critically evaluate AI-generated outputs, identify errors, and validate results before relying on them in professional finance work.

docs.title: Understanding the Language of Business
docs.text:
Accounting is often described as the language of business.

Every company, regardless of size or industry, uses accounting to record:

  • What it owns
  • What it owes
  • How it generates money
  • How it uses resources

Before analysing companies, building financial models, or applying AI to finance workflows, a strong understanding of accounting fundamentals is essential.

docs.title: Why Accounting Matters in Finance
docs.text:
Finance professionals use accounting information every day.

Examples:

docs.title: Investment Banking
docs.text:
Analysts use accounting information to:

  • Build financial models
  • Analyse company performance
  • Value businesses
  • Prepare transaction materials

docs.title: Corporate Finance
docs.text:
Teams use accounting information to:

  • Track profitability
  • Manage budgets
  • Forecast future performance

docs.title: Investment Research
docs.text:
Analysts use accounting information to:

  • Evaluate companies
  • Identify trends
  • Develop investment opinions

docs.title: The Three Financial Statements
docs.text: The foundation of corporate accounting is the relationship between three statements:

docs.title: 1. Income Statement
docs.text:
Answers:

Is the company profitable?

The Income Statement shows:

  • Revenue
  • Expenses
  • Profit

Basic relationship:

Revenue - Expenses = Profit

docs.title: 2. Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Answers:

What does the company own and owe?

The Balance Sheet shows:

docs.title: Assets
docs.text:
Resources controlled by the company.

Examples:

  • Cash
  • Inventory
  • Property
  • Equipment

docs.title: Liabilities
docs.text:
Obligations owed to others.

Examples:

  • Loans
  • Accounts payable

docs.title: Equity
docs.text:
The owners' claim on the business.

The fundamental equation:

Assets = Liabilities + Equity

docs.title: 3. Cash Flow Statement
docs.text:
Answers:

Where did cash come from and where did it go?

The Cash Flow Statement explains movement in cash through:

docs.title: Operating Activities
docs.text: Cash generated from business operations.

docs.title: Investing Activities
docs.text:
Cash used for investments.

Examples:

  • Buying equipment
  • Acquiring companies

docs.title: Financing Activities
docs.text:
Cash related to funding.

Examples:

  • Raising debt
  • Issuing shares
  • Paying dividends

docs.title: How The Statements Connect
docs.text:
The three statements are not independent.

They tell one connected story.

Example:

A company makes a sale.

docs.title: Income Statement
docs.text:
Revenue increases.

↓

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Accounts receivable increases.

↓

docs.title: Cash Flow Statement
docs.text:
Cash may increase later when customers pay.

Understanding these connections is the foundation of financial modelling.

docs.title: Accounting Concepts Every Finance Professional Should Know
Revenue Recognition
docs.text:
Revenue is recorded when it is earned, not necessarily when cash is received.

Example:

A company completes a $100,000 project in December but receives payment in February.

The revenue belongs to December.

docs.title: Accrual Accounting
docs.text:
Accrual accounting records economic activity when it occurs.

This differs from cash accounting, which records transactions only when money moves.

docs.title: Depreciation
docs.text:
Depreciation represents the allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life.

Example:

A company purchases equipment for $100,000.

Instead of recording the entire cost immediately, accounting spreads the expense over several years.

docs.title: Working Capital
docs.text:
Working capital measures short-term operating efficiency.

Common components:

  • Accounts Receivable
  • Inventory
  • Accounts Payable

A company can be profitable but still face cash problems if working capital is poorly managed.

docs.title: Accounting and Artificial Intelligence
docs.text:
AI can enhance accounting workflows by helping professionals:

  • Analyse financial statements
  • Identify unusual trends
  • Automate reporting
  • Summarise disclosures
  • Detect anomalies

However:

AI can process information.

Finance professionals provide:

  • Judgment
  • Context
  • Business understanding

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this module, you should understand:

✅ Why accounting matters in finance ✅ The purpose of the three financial statements ✅ How statements connect ✅ Core accounting concepts ✅ Why accounting knowledge is essential for AI-enabled finance professionals

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Continue your learning journey:

  1. Financial Statements
  2. Corporate Finance
  3. Valuation
  4. Financial Modelling
  5. AI Applications in Finance

docs.title: About the Breville Group Case Study
Learning Finance Through a Real Business
docs.text:
Theory is essential, but finance is ultimately about understanding real businesses.

The Breville Group case study is designed to bridge the gap between finance concepts and real-world application. Rather than learning accounting, financial modelling, or valuation in isolation, you'll apply these skills to the publicly available financial information of a listed company.

Throughout this case study, you'll analyse Breville Group using the same types of documents and analytical techniques employed by finance professionals in investment banking, equity research, corporate finance, consulting, and investment management.

docs.title: Why Breville Group?
docs.text:
Breville Group provides an excellent learning case because it is:

  • A publicly listed company with comprehensive annual reports
  • An internationally recognised consumer products business
  • A company with operations across multiple geographic markets
  • A business with clear financial disclosures and management commentary
  • Suitable for applying accounting, financial modelling, valuation, and AI-assisted analysis

Its annual reports provide enough complexity to challenge learners while remaining accessible to those developing their finance skills.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By completing this case study, you will learn how to:

  • Read and navigate a professional annual report
  • Understand how a business creates value
  • Analyse financial statements in context
  • Identify key business drivers and risks
  • Build structured financial models
  • Apply valuation techniques
  • Use AI responsibly to support financial analysis

The objective is not simply to understand Breville Group, but to develop a repeatable framework for analysing any company.

docs.title: The Learning Process
docs.text: The case study follows the same sequence that many finance professionals use when analysing a company.

docs.title: Step 1 – Understand the Business
docs.text:
Before analysing numbers, understand:

  • What products does the company sell?
  • Who are its customers?
  • Which markets does it operate in?
  • How does it generate revenue?
  • What competitive advantages does it have?

Without understanding the business, financial analysis becomes an exercise in interpreting numbers without context.

docs.title: Step 2 – Read the Annual Report
docs.text:
The annual report provides the foundation for your analysis.

Focus on:

  • Chairman's Letter
  • CEO's Review
  • Operating Review
  • Financial Statements
  • Notes to the Accounts
  • Risk Management
  • Corporate Governance

Understanding management's narrative is just as important as understanding the financial statements.

docs.title: Step 3 – Analyse the Financial Statements
docs.text:
Use the Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow Statement to answer questions such as:

  • Is revenue growing?
  • Are margins improving?
  • Is cash generation strong?
  • How efficiently is capital being deployed?
  • What risks are emerging?

The goal is to understand the story behind the numbers.

docs.title: Step 4 – Build Financial Models
docs.text:
Once historical performance is understood, the next step is to build a structured financial model.

This allows you to:

  • Forecast future performance
  • Test assumptions
  • Evaluate different scenarios
  • Estimate company value

Financial models transform historical information into decision-making tools.

docs.title: Step 5 – Apply Artificial Intelligence
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence can accelerate many parts of the analytical process.

Examples include:

  • Summarising annual reports
  • Extracting financial information
  • Comparing historical performance
  • Identifying trends
  • Generating first drafts of research

However, AI should support—not replace—professional judgement.

Every AI-generated insight should be verified against the original source documents.

docs.title: Expected Outcomes
docs.text:
By the end of this case study, you should be able to:

  • Navigate a professional annual report with confidence
  • Explain how Breville Group creates value
  • Interpret financial statements
  • Identify business strengths and risks
  • Build financial models from public information
  • Apply AI responsibly within a finance workflow

More importantly, you'll develop a structured process that can be applied to virtually any listed company.

docs.title: About the Annual Report
docs.text:
The annual report used throughout this case study is publicly available and remains the primary source of information.

Whenever possible, learners are encouraged to refer back to the original document rather than relying solely on summaries or AI-generated explanations.

Professional finance begins with primary sources.

docs.title: A Note on AI
docs.text:
One of the goals of AI Finance Lab is to demonstrate how artificial intelligence can improve productivity without compromising analytical rigour.

Throughout this case study, AI will be used to:

  • accelerate research,
  • organise information,
  • generate first drafts,
  • highlight potential insights.

The final interpretation, conclusions, and recommendations remain the responsibility of the finance professional.

The strongest analysts are not those who rely most heavily on AI.

They are those who combine strong financial knowledge with thoughtful use of AI.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text:
Continue to the Breville Group Annual Report and begin analysing the business from first principles.

Remember:

Great financial analysis begins with understanding the business—not the spreadsheet.

docs.title: AI Model Assessment & Walkthrough
docs.text: Perspective: Investment Banking Associate (M&A / ECM)

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
This is a first-pass AI-generated financial model.

If I received this from a first-year analyst after telling them, "Build me a quick operating model from the annual report," I would say the model is approximately 50–55% complete.

The structure is clean.

The statements link correctly.

The model is readable.

The assumptions are obvious.

However...

This is not yet an institutional-quality investment banking model.

The biggest issue is that the AI has produced what looks like a mathematical model rather than an economic model.

The formulas work.

The business logic often doesn't.

That distinction is one of the biggest lessons every finance professional eventually learns.

docs.title: Overall Score
docs.text: Category Score Structure 9/10 Formatting 8/10 Formula Consistency 8/10 Accounting Accuracy 7/10 Forecast Methodology 5/10 Model Flexibility 5/10 Banking Readiness 6/10

docs.title: Overall Rating
docs.text:
6/10

A strong AI-generated draft.

Not yet ready for client delivery.

docs.title: First Impression
docs.text:
Within about thirty seconds, an Investment Banking Associate would notice several positive things.

✅ Three financial statements are present.

✅ Statements are linked together.

✅ Historical and forecast periods are clearly separated.

✅ Ratios are calculated separately.

✅ The model is readable.

✅ Circular references have been avoided.

These are exactly the kinds of things junior analysts often struggle with.

So the AI deserves genuine credit.

docs.title: 1. Clean Financial Statement Architecture
docs.text:
The model follows the classic investment banking layout.

Income Statement
        ↓
Cash Flow Statement
        ↓
Balance Sheet

This is exactly how most sell-side models are organised.

Simple.

Logical.

Easy to audit.

docs.title: 2. Formula Simplicity
docs.text:
Most formulas are easy to understand.

For example:

Revenue (t) = Revenue (t-1) × (1 + Growth Rate)

Simple.

Transparent.

Auditable.

That's preferable to unnecessarily complicated nested formulas.

docs.title: 3. Statement Linking
docs.text:
Cash flows correctly into the Balance Sheet.

Net Income flows into retained earnings.

The Income Statement drives the Cash Flow Statement.

This is the minimum standard expected in investment banking.

docs.title: 4. Readability
docs.text:
The workbook is easy to navigate.

  • No excessive colour coding
  • No hidden calculations
  • Minimal merged cells
  • Reasonable spacing

Good formatting matters more than beginners realise.

docs.title: Major Weaknesses
docs.text: This is where an Excel workbook becomes a financial model.

docs.title: Problem 1 — Revenue Is Growing Mechanically
docs.text:
The forecast assumes:

Next Year Revenue
=
Previous Year Revenue × (1 + Growth Rate)

That works mathematically.

It tells us nothing about Breville.

An Associate would immediately ask:

Why is revenue growing?

  • New products?
  • Pricing?
  • US expansion?
  • European growth?
  • Premium product mix?
  • FX tailwinds?

The AI never answers these questions.

Revenue simply grows because a percentage says so.

That's acceptable in first-pass modelling...

but it's not accurate forecasting.

docs.title: Better Practice
docs.text:
Revenue should ideally be driven by:

  • Geographic growth
  • Product launches
  • Volume assumptions
  • Pricing assumptions
  • Market share
  • Foreign exchange
  • Historical management guidance

This makes forecasts defendable.

docs.title: Problem 2 — Margins Are Static
docs.text:
Operating margins barely change.

Real businesses don't behave like this.

Breville experiences:

  • Freight volatility
  • Commodity inflation
  • FX movements
  • Supply chain disruptions
  • Operating leverage

A banking model should explain why margins change.

Instead, AI assumes stability - flatline, which is acceptable in a first pass.

However, this removes much of the commercial insight.

docs.title: Problem 3 — Working Capital Forecast Is Oversimplified
docs.text:
Breville is an inventory-heavy consumer products company.

Working capital ought to be one of its biggest valuation drivers.

The AI forecasts:

  • Inventory
  • Receivables
  • Payables

Professional judgement would need to be excercised for the following forecasts:

  • Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO)
  • Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
  • Days Payables Outstanding (DPO)

These appropriate choice of these metrics will reflect the true operational reality.

docs.title: Problem 4 — Capital Expenditure Is Not Operationally Driven
docs.text:
Capex appears to grow smoothly.

A banker immediately asks:

  • Is Breville expanding manufacturing?
  • Opening distribution centres?
  • Investing in automation?
  • Upgrading ERP systems?
  • Increasing warehouse capacity?

Capex should reflect business strategy.

Not simply historical averages.

docs.title: Problem 5 — Depreciation Schedule Missing
docs.text:
Depreciation appears disconnected from asset additions.

Professional models generally build a PP&E schedule.

Opening PP&E
+ Capital Expenditure
− Asset Disposals
= Closing PP&E

Depreciation is then derived from the asset base.

docs.title: Problem 6 — Debt Schedule Missing
docs.text:
This is probably the biggest omission.

Institutional models always include a debt roll-forward.

Opening Debt
+ New Borrowings
− Repayments
= Closing Debt

Interest expense should be calculated from average debt balances.

Without this schedule, interest becomes an assumption rather than an output.

docs.title: Problem 7 — Share Count Schedule Missing
docs.text:
A valuation model eventually needs:

  • Shares Outstanding
  • Diluted Shares
  • Employee Options
  • Share Buybacks
  • EPS
  • Diluted EPS

None of these schedules currently exist.

docs.title: Problem 8 — Missing Supporting Schedules
docs.text:
Several supporting schedules are absent.

  • Working Capital
  • PP&E
  • Debt
  • Equity
  • Intangibles
  • Leases
  • Share Capital

These schedules improve transparency and reduce modelling errors.

docs.title: Problem 9 — No Scenario Analysis
docs.text:
Investment banking models almost always include:

  • Base Case
  • Bull Case
  • Bear Case
  • Sensitivity Analysis
  • Management Case

The current model contains only a single forecast.

docs.title: AI Hallucinations to Watch For
docs.text:
One lesson every analyst should learn:

Never trust AI simply because the formulas calculate.

Always ask:

"Does this make economic sense?"

Common AI mistakes include:

  • Hardcoded assumptions inside formulas
  • Growth continuing indefinitely
  • Cash increasing despite negative free cash flow
  • Debt disappearing unexpectedly
  • Unrealistically stable margins
  • Circular accounting logic
  • Assets growing without investment

Fortunately, this model avoids many catastrophic errors.

Every assumption still requires professional validation.

docs.title: If I Were Reviewing an Analyst's Work
docs.text: These would be my review comments.

docs.title: Comment 1
docs.text:
Build a proper revenue bridge.

Forecast the business.

What is the concensus growth estimate? Footnote your sources.

docs.title: Comment 2
docs.text:
Separate assumptions from calculations.

Every key driver should live in one assumptions section.

docs.title: Comment 3
docs.text:
Build proper supporting schedules.

  • Debt
  • PP&E
  • Working Capital

docs.title: Comment 4
docs.text:
Replace percentage assumptions with operational drivers wherever possible.

Justify flatlines with a comment.

docs.title: Comment 5
docs.text:
Double-check your Balance Sheet Check.

Please reconcile:

Assets = Liabilities + Equity

The difference should always equal zero.

docs.title: Comment 6
docs.text:
Add scenario controls.

"What happens if revenue slows?"

The model should answer that question with one click.

docs.title: What AI Gets Right
docs.text:
This model demonstrates something important.

Without guidance on data sources and formats, AI is already capable of:

  • Extracting (scraping to be more precise) financial data
  • Linking statements
  • Building financial models
  • Forecasting mechanically
  • Producing clean Excel structures

That alone can save analysts hours.

docs.title: What AI Cannot Yet Replace
docs.text:
AI still cannot replace:

  • Commercial judgement
  • Industry understanding
  • Management interpretation
  • Forecast rationale
  • Business intuition
  • Valuation judgement

Those remain fundamentally human skills.

docs.title: Final Verdict
docs.text:
As an Investment Banker, I would accept this workbook as a starting point, not a finished deliverable.

It eliminates approximately 50–60% of the repetitive Excel work traditionally performed by junior analysts.

The remaining 40–50%—transforming the spreadsheet into an institutional-quality financial model—still requires professional judgement, commercial thinking and rigorous modelling discipline.

The biggest takeaway is not that AI replaces financial modelling.

Rather...

AI changes where analysts spend their time.

Less time building rows.

Less time writing formulas.

More time understanding businesses.

More time validating assumptions.

More time generating investment insight.

docs.title: AI Model Assessment Summary
docs.text:
What you're looking at is a first draft generated with AI. The objective wasn't to build a perfect model, but to see how far AI can take us before human judgement becomes essential.

The AI has correctly structured the three financial statements, linked them together, and projected the business into the future. That's already a meaningful productivity gain. Tasks that might take a junior analyst several hours can now be completed in minutes.

But here's the key lesson: a spreadsheet that calculates is not necessarily a financial model that explains a business.

Notice how revenue is forecast using a simple growth assumption. That's mathematically correct, but an investment banker wants to understand why revenue is growing. Is it pricing? Product launches? Geographic expansion? FX? Consumer demand? Those are business questions—not spreadsheet questions.

The same applies to margins, working capital and capital expenditure. AI has extrapolated historical trends, but it doesn't yet understand management strategy or operational realities. That's where human analysis creates value.

Think of AI as your first-year analyst. It can assemble the framework quickly, perform repetitive tasks consistently and eliminate much of the manual work. But every assumption still needs to be challenged.

Our role as finance professionals is no longer just building models. It's reviewing them, improving them, stress-testing them and ensuring every number reflects the economics of the business rather than simply extending historical trends.

That's exactly the philosophy behind the AI Finance Lab. We use AI to automate the mechanics so we can spend more time on judgement, valuation and investment insight.

docs.title: AI Walkthrough - DCF Opinion Analysis
docs.text:
Imagine you are an Equity Research Analyst preparing an initiation report on Breville Group.

Your portfolio manager asks a simple question:

What is Breville worth based on its future cash-generating ability?

Before opening Excel, determine the factors that will ultimately drive Breville's intrinsic value.

docs.title: Step 1 — Understand the Business
docs.text:
A DCF begins with understanding how a company creates value.

Read the Breville FY2025 Annual Report and identify the following.

Question Breville Observation Why It Matters How does Breville make money? Premium small domestic appliances sold across multiple global markets. Revenue growth begins with understanding the business model. What differentiates Breville? Strong brand recognition, product innovation and premium positioning. Competitive advantages support long-term profitability. Which regions drive revenue? North America represents the largest contributor, supported by Australia, New Zealand and Europe. Geographic mix influences future growth opportunities and risk. What are management's strategic priorities? Continued international expansion, innovation and premium product development. Strategy provides clues about future financial performance.

docs.title: Initial Assessment
docs.text:
Breville appears to possess several characteristics associated with long-term value creation:

  • Premium global brands.
  • Strong product innovation.
  • International growth opportunities.
  • Established retail relationships.
  • Attractive operating margins.

These characteristics suggest the business has the potential to generate sustainable future cash flows.

docs.title: Step 2 — Identify the Key Value Drivers
docs.text:
Every DCF is driven by a relatively small number of assumptions.

Using the annual report, identify the factors most likely to influence Breville's future cash flows.

Driver Why It Matters Revenue Growth Determines the future scale of the business. Gross Margin Reflects pricing power and operational efficiency. Operating Expenses Determines operating leverage as the company grows. Capital Expenditure Cash required to support future expansion. Working Capital Cash tied up in inventory and receivables. Tax Rate Influences after-tax cash generation.

These drivers form the foundation of every financial forecast.

docs.title: Step 3 — Assess Competitive Advantages
docs.text:
Businesses create value when they earn returns above their cost of capital.

Review the annual report and identify evidence of durable competitive advantages.

Possible observations include:

Competitive Advantage Evidence from the Annual Report Premium Brand Strong consumer recognition and premium positioning. Product Innovation Ongoing investment in research and product development. Distribution Network Long-standing relationships with major retailers globally. Pricing Power Premium product mix supporting attractive margins.

Competitive advantages influence both future growth and profitability.

docs.title: Step 4 — Evaluate Business Risks
docs.text:
A DCF must balance optimism with realism.

Identify the risks that could reduce Breville's future cash flows.

Examples include:

Risk Potential Financial Impact Consumer spending slowdown Reduced revenue growth. Supply chain disruption Higher operating costs and lower margins. Currency movements Earnings volatility across international markets. Increased competition Pressure on pricing and market share. Product execution Delays in innovation reducing future growth.

These risks should influence both forecast assumptions and the discount rate.

docs.title: Step 5 — Estimate Long-Term Growth
docs.text:
A DCF is not simply about forecasting the next few years.

It also requires forming a view on Breville's long-term prospects.

Consider:

  • Can the company continue expanding internationally?
  • Are premium kitchen appliances likely to remain an attractive market?
  • Does management have a credible long-term strategy?
  • Can Breville maintain its competitive advantages over the next decade?

Long-term growth should be ambitious enough to reflect opportunity but conservative enough to remain credible.

docs.title: Step 6 — Consider the Appropriate Discount Rate
docs.text:
Investors require compensation for taking risk.

Before estimating a discount rate, consider:

Question Observation Is Breville financially stable? Relatively healthy balance sheet and established operations. Is demand cyclical? Consumer discretionary spending introduces economic sensitivity. Does the company operate internationally? Global operations introduce foreign exchange and geopolitical risk. Is the business mature or high-growth? Established business with continued international expansion opportunities.

These factors influence the company's overall risk profile and, ultimately, its cost of capital.

docs.title: Step 7 — Does Breville Create Value?
docs.text:
After reviewing the business qualitatively, ask yourself:

  • Does Breville appear capable of generating growing cash flows?
  • Are those cash flows likely to be sustainable?
  • Does the company possess durable competitive advantages?
  • Are the identified risks manageable?

If the answer to these questions is largely "yes", then Breville may justify a premium valuation.

docs.title: Positive Indicators
docs.text:
• Premium global consumer brand.
  • Strong history of product innovation.
  • Attractive operating margins.
  • International growth opportunities.
  • Healthy cash generation.
  • Experienced management team.

docs.title: Key Risks
docs.text:
• Consumer discretionary exposure.
  • Dependence on successful product launches.
  • Supply chain and inventory management.
  • Foreign exchange volatility.
  • Competitive pressure from global appliance manufacturers.

docs.title: Analyst's Conclusion
docs.text:
Based solely on the qualitative review of the annual report:

Breville appears to be a high-quality business capable of generating sustainable long-term cash flows.

However:

Quality alone does not determine value.

A great business can still be a poor investment if purchased at too high a price.

The next step is therefore not to issue a Buy or Sell recommendation.

Instead:

Build a Discounted Cash Flow model to estimate Breville's intrinsic value and compare that value with the current market price.

Only then can an informed investment recommendation be made.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
A DCF is often described as a valuation model.

In reality, it is a framework for thinking.

It forces analysts to answer difficult questions about:

  • Growth
  • Profitability
  • Capital allocation
  • Risk
  • Competitive advantage
  • Long-term strategy

The spreadsheet simply converts those assumptions into numbers.

The real work happens long before the first formula is written.

docs.title: AI Walkthrough - Financial Model Opinion Analysis
docs.text:
Imagine you have just joined an Investment Banking team.

Your Senior Associate has handed you Breville's FY2025 Annual Report and given you your first assignment.

Build a financial model that accurately represents how Breville operates as a business.

Before opening Excel, answer the question every analyst should ask:

What are the economic drivers of this business?

A financial model is not a collection of formulas.

It is a representation of how a company creates value.

docs.title: Step 1 — Understand the Business
docs.text:
Begin by reading the Annual Report from start to finish.

Focus on understanding the business before thinking about numbers.

Question Breville Observation Why It Matters What does Breville sell? Premium small domestic kitchen appliances including coffee machines, ovens, blenders and food preparation products. Understanding the products explains how revenue is generated. Who are Breville's customers? Consumers seeking premium kitchen appliances across global markets. Customer demographics influence pricing power and long-term demand. Where does Breville operate? Australia, New Zealand, North America, Europe and selected international markets. Geographic diversification affects both growth opportunities and risk. How does Breville compete? Innovation, premium product design, strong branding and retail partnerships. Competitive advantages influence profitability and future performance.

docs.title: Initial Assessment
docs.text:
Breville is not simply a designer, developer, and distributor.

It is a premium consumer products company that competes through:

  • Innovation
  • Brand strength
  • Product quality
  • Distribution
  • Customer experience

Understanding these characteristics is the first step in building a meaningful financial model.

docs.title: Step 2 — Identify the Business Drivers
docs.text:
Every financial model begins with identifying the variables that drive performance.

Using the annual report, identify the key drivers of Breville's business.

Driver Why It Matters Revenue Growth Expands the overall size of the business. Product Mix Premium products generally support higher margins. Gross Margin Indicates pricing power and manufacturing efficiency. Operating Expenses Reflect the cost of growing the business. Capital Expenditure Supports future innovation and expansion. Working Capital Inventory and receivables determine how efficiently cash is managed.

These assumptions will ultimately drive every forecast in the financial model.

docs.title: Step 3 — Read Breville's Financial Statements Like an Analyst
docs.text:
Now that you understand Breville's business model, it's time to connect that understanding to the numbers.

As you read each financial statement, don't just ask "What changed?"

Instead ask:

"Why did it change?"

Every number in the financial statements tells part of Breville's story.

docs.title: Income Statement — How Does Breville Make Money?
docs.text:
Start with the Income Statement.

As you read through it, look for evidence of Breville's business strategy.

Question What to Look For Is revenue growing? Has Breville successfully expanded into new markets or launched successful products? How stable are gross margins? Does premium pricing allow Breville to maintain healthy margins despite inflation or higher manufacturing costs? Are operating expenses increasing? Is the company investing more heavily in marketing, R&D or international expansion? Is profitability improving? Are investments translating into stronger EBIT and Net Profit over time?

Rather than memorising the numbers, try to explain why they changed.

docs.title: Balance Sheet — Where Has Breville Invested?
docs.text:
Next, review the Balance Sheet.

Think of it as a snapshot of how management has deployed capital.

Question What to Look For Is inventory increasing? Is Breville preparing for future demand, or could inventory be building too quickly? Are receivables growing faster than sales? Could customers be taking longer to pay? Has cash increased? Is the company generating more cash than it needs for operations? Are borrowings increasing? Is growth being financed through debt or internally generated cash? Is shareholders' equity increasing? Is the business creating value over time?

The Balance Sheet tells you how management is funding future growth.

docs.title: Cash Flow Statement — Does Breville Turn Profit Into Cash?
docs.text:
Finally, analyse the Cash Flow Statement.

Remember:

Revenue is important. Profit is better. Cash is essential.

Focus on the following questions.

Question Why It Matters Is operating cash flow consistently positive? Strong operating cash flow indicates the underlying business is healthy. How much cash is reinvested into the business? Capital expenditure reveals management's commitment to future growth. Does Breville generate free cash flow? Free cash flow ultimately drives company valuation. How is excess cash being used? Debt repayment, dividends, acquisitions or share buybacks all tell different stories about management's priorities.

A company can report strong accounting profits while generating weak cash flow.

Professional analysts always reconcile the two.

docs.title: Putting the Three Statements Together
docs.text:
Now connect the dots.

Suppose Breville launches a successful new espresso machine.

How would that flow through the financial statements?

Business Event Financial Impact Strong product sales Revenue increases on the Income Statement. Higher manufacturing volumes Cost of Sales increases, but operating leverage may improve margins. Higher Net Profit Retained Earnings increase on the Balance Sheet. Increased customer demand Inventory may temporarily decline before being replenished. Customers pay for purchases Operating Cash Flow increases and Cash on the Balance Sheet grows.

This exercise illustrates an important principle:

Financial statements are not independent reports—they are different perspectives on the same business.

As an analyst, your job is to understand the commercial events that connect them.

Before building your financial model, ask yourself:

  • What are Breville's strongest financial trends?
  • Which metrics concern you most?
  • What assumptions would you make if forecasting the next five years?

These observations will become the foundation of every projection you build.

docs.title: Step 4 — Follow the Story Through the Statements
docs.text:
One of the most valuable exercises is tracing how one business event flows through all three financial statements.

For example:

Business Event Financial Impact Higher product sales Revenue increases on the Income Statement. Higher profits Net Income increases. Higher retained earnings Equity increases on the Balance Sheet. Customer payments received Cash increases on the Cash Flow Statement and Balance Sheet.

This interconnectedness is the foundation of every professional financial model.

docs.title: Step 5 — Build Forecast Assumptions
docs.text:
Financial models are not built by predicting the future.

They are built by making informed assumptions.

Using the annual report, consider:

Question Example Considerations Can revenue continue growing? International expansion, new product launches and consumer demand. Can margins improve? Product mix, pricing power and operational efficiency. Will operating expenses grow? Investment in people, marketing and innovation. How much capital investment is required? Manufacturing, technology and product development.

Every forecast should have a logical business explanation.

docs.title: Step 6 — Validate the Model
docs.text:
Before relying on any model, ensure it behaves as expected.

Ask yourself:

  • Do the three financial statements remain linked?
  • Does the Balance Sheet balance?
  • Are cash flows reasonable?
  • Do forecast trends align with management commentary?
  • Are assumptions internally consistent?

A model that balances but produces unrealistic forecasts is still a poor model.

Validation requires both technical accuracy and commercial judgement.

docs.title: Step 7 — What Does the Model Tell Us?
docs.text:
Once the model is complete, it becomes more than a forecasting tool.

It becomes a framework for asking better questions.

For example:

  • What happens if revenue growth slows?
  • How sensitive are profits to gross margin changes?
  • Can the company fund future expansion internally?
  • Is working capital becoming more efficient?
  • How resilient is the business during an economic slowdown?

These are the questions that finance professionals answer every day.

docs.title: Preliminary Analyst View
docs.text: Based on the qualitative review of the FY2025 Annual Report:

docs.title: Business Strengths
docs.text:
• Premium global brand.
  • Strong culture of innovation.
  • Diversified geographic footprint.
  • Attractive operating margins.
  • Consistent investment in product development.
  • Healthy long-term growth opportunities.

docs.title: Key Modelling Considerations
docs.text:
• Consumer discretionary demand.
  • Foreign exchange exposure.
  • Inventory management.
  • Product launch execution.
  • Supply chain resilience.
  • Long-term international expansion.

These factors should inform every forecast assumption within the financial model.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Many beginners think financial modelling is about Excel.

Professional analysts know otherwise.

A financial model is a structured representation of how a business works.

Excel is simply the tool used to communicate that understanding.

If you understand the business well, building the spreadsheet becomes relatively straightforward.

If you do not understand the business, even the most sophisticated spreadsheet will produce unreliable results.

docs.title: Takeaway
docs.text:
The purpose of a Three-Statement Financial Model is not to predict the future perfectly.

Its purpose is to create a structured framework for understanding how decisions, assumptions and business performance affect a company's financial outcomes.

Every valuation model—DCF, LBO and Merger Model—begins with a well-constructed three-statement financial model.

Without it, every subsequent analysis rests on an unstable foundation.

Build the business first. Everything else follows.

docs.title: AI Walkthrough - LBO Strategic Analysis
docs.text:
Imagine you are an Investment Associate, tasked with preparing an initial investment committee paper.

Your Managing Director has asked one question:

Should KKR acquire Breville Group through a take private transaction?

Before opening Excel, evaluate whether Breville exhibits the characteristics of an attractive private equity investment.

docs.title: Step 1 — Investment Thesis
docs.text:
Read the Breville FY2025 Annual Report.

Start by identifying what makes Breville attractive.

Question Breville Observation Why It Matters for Private Equity Does the company operate in an attractive industry? Premium small kitchen appliances continue to benefit from premiumisation and increasing home cooking trends. Attractive industries generally produce more predictable long-term returns. Does Breville have strong brands? Breville has developed premium consumer brands with significant market recognition. Strong brands provide pricing power and reduce competitive pressure. Does the company generate recurring cash flow? Products generate repeat purchases through product upgrades and accessories, although demand remains consumer discretionary. Predictable cash flow is essential for servicing acquisition debt. Does management have a track record of execution? Long history of international expansion and product innovation. Strong management reduces execution risk during ownership.

docs.title: Initial Assessment
docs.text:
Breville appears to possess several characteristics commonly sought by private equity investors:

  • Premium consumer brand.
  • Strong international presence.
  • Attractive operating margins.
  • Consistent profitability.
  • Opportunities for operational improvement.

However, a good company does not automatically make a good LBO.

The next question is whether the business can support leverage.

docs.title: Step 2 — Can the Business Support Debt?
docs.text:
An LBO relies heavily on debt financing.

Using the annual report, consider:

Question Observation Does the company consistently generate operating cash flow? Positive operating cash flows over multiple years indicate the business can service debt. Is capital expenditure manageable? Moderate ongoing investment requirements leave more cash available for debt repayment. Does the company require significant working capital investment? Large working capital requirements reduce free cash available for lenders. Are earnings volatile? Highly cyclical earnings increase financial risk under leverage.

docs.title: Initial Assessment
docs.text:
Breville's relatively stable cash generation suggests that moderate leverage may be feasible.

However:

  • Consumer demand remains discretionary.
  • Earnings may weaken during economic slowdowns.
  • Inventory management is critical.

These factors would influence the amount of debt lenders are willing to provide.

docs.title: Step 3 — Value Creation Opportunities
docs.text:
Private equity firms create value after acquisition.

Identify opportunities that could increase enterprise value during a typical five-year holding period.

Opportunity Potential Value Creation Margin Improvement Streamline procurement and manufacturing to improve operating margins. Supply Chain Optimisation Reduce logistics costs and improve inventory management. Geographic Expansion Accelerate growth in underpenetrated international markets. Product Innovation Continue launching premium products with higher gross margins. Digital Sales Increase direct-to-consumer sales to improve profitability.

These initiatives should increase EBITDA over time, creating value even before considering leverage.

docs.title: Step 4 — Key Investment Risks
docs.text:
Private equity investors spend as much time evaluating downside risks as upside opportunities.

Risk Potential Impact Consumer spending slowdown Lower revenue and reduced cash flow available for debt service. Supply chain disruption Increased costs and reduced margins. Execution risk Operational improvements may not materialise. High purchase price Lower investor returns despite operational success. Rising interest rates Increased financing costs reduce equity returns.

The greatest risk in many LBOs is not the company.

It is paying too much for the company.

docs.title: Step 5 — Potential Exit Strategy
docs.text:
Private equity investments are temporary.

Before acquiring a company, investors ask:

Who will buy this business in five years?

Potential exits include:

Exit Route Assessment Strategic Buyer Companies such as De'Longhi or Whirlpool could acquire Breville to strengthen their premium appliance portfolio. Secondary Buyout Another private equity firm may purchase the business after operational improvements. Public Markets A relisting could provide an exit if valuation remains attractive.

Having multiple credible exit options generally improves investment attractiveness.

docs.title: Investment Strengths
docs.text:
• Premium global consumer brand.
  • Strong cash generation.
  • International growth opportunities.
  • Attractive operating margins.
  • Multiple operational improvement opportunities.
  • Several credible exit options.

docs.title: Key Concerns
docs.text:
• Consumer discretionary exposure.
  • Potential cyclicality during economic downturns.
  • Purchase price discipline.
  • Interest rate environment.
  • Inventory and supply chain management.

docs.title: Investment Committee Recommendation
docs.text:
Based solely on the qualitative review of the annual report:

Breville appears to possess many characteristics of an attractive private equity investment.

However, the recommendation is not yet to acquire the company.

Instead:

Proceed to detailed LBO modelling to determine whether the expected investor returns justify the proposed purchase price.

Only after analysing:

  • Purchase multiple
  • Debt capacity
  • Cash flow generation
  • Debt repayment
  • Exit valuation
  • Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
  • Money Multiple (MoM)

can the investment committee make an informed acquisition decision.

A strong business does not necessarily produce an attractive investment.

The purchase price ultimately determines whether value is created.

docs.title: AI Walkthrough - Merger Strategic Analysis
docs.text:
In this exercise, we'll step into the shoes of a banker advising De'Longhi S.p.A., one of Breville's largest global competitors.

The objective is not to build a merger model immediately.

Instead, begin by answering the question every deal team asks before opening Excel:

Should De'Longhi acquire Breville?

docs.title: Step 1 — Strategic Rationale
docs.text:
Read the Breville FY2025 Annual Report.

Imagine you are preparing a discussion paper for De'Longhi's Board.

Consider the following observations.

Question Breville Observation Potential Value to De'Longhi Does Breville own strong brands? Breville has built premium kitchen appliance brands with strong consumer recognition across Australia, North America and Europe. Expands De'Longhi's premium brand portfolio while strengthening its position in higher-margin kitchen appliances. Does Breville operate in attractive markets? North America contributes the majority of revenue and continues to be Breville's largest growth market. Accelerates De'Longhi's expansion in the US without building distribution organically. Does Breville have differentiated products? Continuous investment in product innovation and premium consumer experiences. Provides complementary products and strengthens innovation capabilities. Does Breville possess attractive distribution? Extensive retail partnerships and growing direct-to-consumer capabilities. Immediate access to established distribution channels and customer relationships.

docs.title: Initial Assessment
docs.text:
From a strategic perspective, the acquisition appears attractive because it would allow De'Longhi to:

  • Strengthen its premium appliance portfolio.
  • Increase exposure to North America.
  • Expand innovation capabilities.
  • Increase scale in small domestic appliances.

Notice that this conclusion comes from understanding the business—not from building a financial model.

docs.title: Step 2 — Revenue Synergies
docs.text:
Now ask:

How could the combined company generate more revenue than the two businesses separately?

Potential revenue synergies include:

Opportunity Example Cross-selling Selling Breville espresso machines through De'Longhi's established European distribution network. Distribution Introducing De'Longhi products into Breville's strongest North American retail relationships. Geographic Expansion Accelerating Breville's expansion into markets where De'Longhi already has a strong presence. Customer Base Leveraging combined customer loyalty programmes and direct-to-consumer channels. Product Innovation Combining engineering and product development teams to launch new premium products faster.

Revenue synergies are often difficult to quantify but can become significant over the long term.

docs.title: Step 3 — Cost Synergies
docs.text:
Next, identify where operating costs could be reduced.

Examples include:

Function Potential Synergy Procurement Increased purchasing power with suppliers. Manufacturing Rationalising production facilities and improving capacity utilisation. Logistics Consolidating warehouses and freight networks. Corporate Functions Combining Finance, HR, IT and Legal teams. Marketing Shared global campaigns and reduced duplication.

Unlike revenue synergies, cost synergies are generally easier to estimate and are often the primary source of value creation in acquisitions.

docs.title: Step 4 — Integration Challenges
docs.text:
Every acquisition introduces execution risk.

Potential integration challenges include:

Challenge Why It Matters Culture Both companies have different management styles and corporate cultures. Brand Positioning Maintaining the premium identity of both brands while avoiding customer confusion. Technology Integrating ERP systems, reporting processes and data infrastructure. Supply Chain Aligning manufacturing locations, suppliers and inventory management. Product Overlap Rationalising overlapping product lines without losing customers.

The quality of integration often determines whether expected synergies are realised.

docs.title: Step 5 — Risks That Could Destroy Value
docs.text:
Even strategically attractive acquisitions can fail.

Potential risks include:

Risk Potential Impact Paying too much Future returns fail to justify the acquisition premium. Synergies not realised Expected cost savings never materialise. Integration delays Operational disruption reduces profitability. Customer attrition Existing customers switch to competitors during integration. Management distraction Leadership focus shifts from operating the business to integrating the acquisition.

Investment bankers spend significant time evaluating these risks before recommending a transaction.

docs.title: Preliminary Investment View
docs.text:
Based solely on the qualitative analysis of the annual report, the acquisition appears strategically compelling.

Strengths

  • Highly complementary product portfolios.
  • Strong geographic fit.
  • Opportunity to expand distribution.
  • Significant procurement and operating scale benefits.
  • Attractive premium consumer brands.

Key Risks

  • Execution of integration.
  • Maintaining both brand identities.
  • Achieving projected synergies.
  • Paying an appropriate acquisition premium.

At this stage, the recommendation is not:

"De'Longhi should acquire Breville."

Instead, the recommendation is:

"The transaction appears strategically attractive and warrants detailed financial analysis."

This is precisely where merger modelling begins.

The next step will require determining whether the strategic rationale is supported by the financial evidence.

docs.title: Understanding the Story Behind Every Business
docs.text:
Every company tells a story.

That story is captured through three interconnected financial statements:

  1. Income Statement
  2. Balance Sheet
  3. Cash Flow Statement

Together, these statements answer three fundamental questions:

Question Financial Statement Is the company making money? Income Statement What does the company own and owe? Balance Sheet Where is cash coming from and going? Cash Flow Statement

A finance professional's job is not just reading numbers.

It is understanding the business story behind those numbers.

docs.title: "Is this business profitable?"
docs.text:
The Income Statement measures company performance over a period of time.

Examples:

  • Month
  • Quarter
  • Year

It shows how much money the company generated and how much it spent.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
Revenue (-) Cost of Goods Sold = Gross Profit

(-) Operating Expenses = Operating Income

(-) Interest & Taxes = Net Income

docs.title: Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue represents money earned from selling products or services.

Examples:

  • A software company selling subscriptions
  • A retailer selling products
  • A consulting firm billing clients

docs.title: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
docs.text:
Costs directly associated with producing goods or delivering services.

Examples:

Manufacturing company: - Raw materials - Factory labour

Retail company: - Cost of inventory purchased

docs.title: Gross Profit
docs.text:
Gross Profit measures profitability after direct costs.

Formula:

Gross Profit = Revenue - COGS

Gross margin:

Gross Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue

A higher gross margin often indicates stronger pricing power or operational efficiency.

docs.title: Operating Expenses
docs.text:
Costs required to run the business.

Examples:

  • Salaries
  • Marketing
  • Rent
  • Technology expenses
  • Administrative costs

docs.title: EBITDA
docs.text:
EBITDA stands for:

Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization

It is often used by investors and bankers to compare operating performance.

Formula:

EBITDA = Operating Income + Depreciation + Amortization

docs.title: "What does the company own and owe?"
docs.text:
Unlike the Income Statement, the Balance Sheet represents a point in time.

Example:

"As of December 31, 2026"

The Balance Sheet follows one fundamental equation:

Assets = Liabilities + Equity

This equation must always balance.

docs.title: Assets
docs.text:
Assets are resources controlled by the company.

Examples:

docs.title: Current Assets
docs.text:
Expected to convert into cash within one year.

Examples:

  • Cash
  • Accounts Receivable
  • Inventory

docs.title: Non-Current Assets
docs.text:
Long-term resources.

Examples:

  • Property
  • Equipment
  • Intangible assets

docs.title: Liabilities
docs.text:
Liabilities represent obligations.

Examples:

docs.title: Current Liabilities
docs.text:
Due within one year.

Examples:

  • Accounts Payable
  • Short-term debt

docs.title: Long-Term Liabilities
docs.text:
Due beyond one year.

Examples:

  • Bank loans
  • Bonds

docs.title: Equity
docs.text:
Equity represents the owners' claim on the company.

Common components:

  • Share capital
  • Retained earnings

A simplified relationship:

Equity = Assets - Liabilities

docs.title: "Profit is an opinion. Cash is reality."
docs.text:
A company can report profits but still run out of cash.

The Cash Flow Statement explains the movement of cash.

It has three sections:

docs.title: Operating Cash Flow
docs.text:
Cash generated from normal business operations.

Examples:

  • Customer payments
  • Supplier payments
  • Employee expenses

docs.title: Investing Cash Flow
docs.text:
Cash related to long-term investments.

Examples:

  • Buying equipment
  • Selling assets
  • Acquisitions

docs.title: Financing Cash Flow
docs.text:
Cash related to funding activities.

Examples:

  • Raising debt
  • Issuing shares
  • Paying dividends

docs.title: How The Three Statements Connect
docs.text:
