Understanding connections between statements is one of the most important skills in finance.

Example:

A company sells $100,000 worth of products on credit.

docs.title: Income Statement
docs.text:
Revenue increases by $100,000.

↓

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Accounts Receivable increases by $100,000.

↓

docs.title: Cash Flow Statement
docs.text:
No cash received yet.

Later, the customer pays.

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Accounts Receivable decreases.

↓

docs.title: Cash Flow Statement
docs.text:
Operating cash flow increases.

This connection is the foundation of financial modelling.

docs.title: Financial Statements Through a Business Lens
docs.text:
Numbers alone do not explain a company.

A finance professional asks:

docs.title: Revenue
docs.text:
• Why is revenue growing?
  • Is growth sustainable?
  • Are customers increasing?

docs.title: Margins
docs.text:
• Is profitability improving?
  • Are costs under control?

docs.title: Assets
docs.text: • Is capital being used efficiently?

docs.title: Cash Flow
docs.text: • Can the company fund itself?

docs.title: Using AI to Analyse Financial Statements
docs.text: AI can help finance professionals:

docs.title: Summarisation
docs.text: Convert hundreds of pages of annual reports into concise insights.

docs.title: Trend Analysis
docs.text:
Identify:

  • Revenue patterns
  • Margin changes
  • Expense trends

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text:
Compare companies across:

  • Growth
  • Profitability
  • Capital efficiency

docs.title: Question Answering
docs.text: Allow analysts to interact with financial documents.

docs.title: But AI Cannot Replace Financial Judgment
docs.text:
AI can identify patterns.

Finance professionals must interpret:

  • Why did margins change?
  • Is growth sustainable?
  • What risks exist?
  • What decisions should management make?

The value comes from combining:

Financial Knowledge + Business Understanding + AI Capability

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this module, you should understand:

✅ The purpose of the three financial statements ✅ How profitability differs from cash generation ✅ How statements connect together ✅ How investors analyse businesses ✅ How AI can enhance financial analysis

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Continue your learning journey:

  1. Corporate Finance Fundamentals
  2. Financial Modelling
  3. Valuation
  4. AI-Powered Finance Workflows

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is one of the most widely used valuation techniques in corporate finance, investment banking, private equity, and equity research.

At its core, a DCF answers a simple question:

What is a business worth today based on the cash it is expected to generate in the future?

Unlike valuation methods that rely on market prices or comparable companies, a DCF estimates a company's intrinsic value by forecasting future cash flows and converting them into today's dollars.

This lesson explains the mechanics behind DCF valuation without focusing on Excel implementation.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
After completing this lesson, you should be able to:

  • Understand the purpose of a DCF valuation.
  • Explain the concept of intrinsic value.
  • Understand why future cash flows must be discounted.
  • Explain the role of Free Cash Flow (FCF).
  • Understand the purpose of WACC.
  • Explain Terminal Value.
  • Recognise the strengths and limitations of DCF valuation.

docs.title: Why Do We Need Valuation?
docs.text:
Every investment decision begins with one question:

What is this business worth?

Investors ask this question when:

  • Buying shares
  • Acquiring companies
  • Raising capital
  • Evaluating investments
  • Assessing strategic opportunities

Price and value are not always the same.

The purpose of valuation is to estimate what a business is truly worth.

docs.title: Intrinsic Value
docs.text:
Intrinsic value represents the estimated economic value of a business based on its future ability to generate cash.

Unlike market price, intrinsic value is independent of investor sentiment.

Future Cash Flows

↓

Present Value

↓

Intrinsic Value

If:

  • Intrinsic Value > Market Price

the business may be undervalued.

If:

  • Intrinsic Value < Market Price

the business may be overvalued.

docs.title: The Time Value of Money
docs.text:
One of the most important principles in finance is:

A dollar today is worth more than a dollar received in the future.

Why?

Because money received today can be invested and earn a return.

For example:

Receiving $100 today is generally more valuable than receiving $100 five years from now.

DCF valuation accounts for this principle by discounting future cash flows back to today's value.

docs.title: What Is Free Cash Flow?
docs.text:
DCF valuation focuses on Free Cash Flow (FCF) rather than accounting profit.

Free Cash Flow represents the cash generated by a business that is available to investors after funding its operations and necessary investments.

Think of Free Cash Flow as:

Cash that the business can distribute without damaging its future operations.

Unlike Net Income, Free Cash Flow reflects actual cash generation.

docs.title: Forecasting Future Cash Flows
docs.text:
The first step in a DCF is forecasting how much cash the business is expected to generate.

Forecasts are based on assumptions such as:

  • Revenue growth
  • Operating margins
  • Capital expenditure
  • Working capital
  • Tax rates

Forecast quality depends entirely on the quality of these assumptions.

docs.title: Why Do We Discount Cash Flows?
docs.text:
Future cash flows are uncertain.

Investors require compensation for:

  • Waiting
  • Inflation
  • Risk
  • Opportunity cost

Discounting adjusts future cash flows to reflect their value today.

The further into the future a cash flow occurs, the lower its present value.

docs.title: Understanding WACC
docs.text:
The discount rate used in most DCF models is the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC).

WACC represents the average return required by all providers of capital.

These include:

  • Equity investors
  • Debt investors

Conceptually:

Higher Risk

↓

Higher Required Return

↓

Higher Discount Rate

↓

Lower Present Value

Companies with higher risk generally have higher discount rates.

docs.title: Forecast Period
docs.text:
A DCF typically forecasts cash flows over a finite period.

Common forecast horizons are:

  • 5 years
  • 7 years
  • 10 years

During this period, analysts estimate annual Free Cash Flow.

docs.title: Terminal Value
docs.text:
Businesses do not stop operating after five years.

Terminal Value estimates the value of all future cash flows beyond the explicit forecast period.

For most companies, Terminal Value represents a significant proportion of total enterprise value.

There are two common approaches:

  • Perpetual Growth Method
  • Exit Multiple Method

Terminal Value should always be interpreted carefully because small assumption changes can have a large impact on valuation.

docs.title: Enterprise Value
docs.text:
The sum of:

  • Present Value of Forecast Cash Flows

plus

  • Present Value of Terminal Value

equals:

Enterprise Value

Enterprise Value represents the value of the entire operating business.

It belongs to all capital providers.

docs.title: From Enterprise Value to Equity Value
docs.text:
Investors ultimately own equity.

To estimate Equity Value:

Enterprise Value

− Net Debt

± Other Adjustments

↓

Equity Value

Dividing Equity Value by shares outstanding produces an estimated intrinsic value per share.

docs.title: Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text:
A DCF is only as reliable as its assumptions.

Professional analysts test how valuation changes when assumptions change.

Common sensitivity variables include:

  • Revenue Growth
  • EBITDA Margin
  • WACC
  • Terminal Growth Rate

Sensitivity analysis helps decision-makers understand valuation uncertainty.

docs.title: Strengths of DCF Valuation
docs.text:
DCF valuation:

  • Focuses on business fundamentals.
  • Estimates intrinsic value.
  • Encourages structured thinking.
  • Can be applied across industries.
  • Makes assumptions transparent.

docs.title: Limitations of DCF Valuation
docs.text:
DCF models depend heavily on assumptions.

Small changes in:

  • Growth
  • Discount Rate
  • Terminal Value

can produce materially different valuations.

For this reason:

A DCF should inform judgement, not replace it.

Professional investors rarely rely on a DCF in isolation.

It is usually considered alongside comparable company analysis, precedent transactions, and qualitative business analysis.

docs.title: Where AI Fits Into DCF Analysis
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence can assist with many stages of DCF preparation.

Examples include:

  • Summarising annual reports
  • Identifying revenue drivers
  • Estimating business risks
  • Generating forecasting assumptions
  • Explaining accounting concepts
  • Reviewing model logic
  • Drafting valuation summaries

However, AI should never determine valuation assumptions without professional review.

Finance professionals remain responsible for:

  • Judgement
  • Validation
  • Critical thinking

docs.title: DCF Workflow in the AI Finance Lab
docs.text:
Throughout the AI Finance Lab, DCF analysis follows this workflow:

Understand the Business

↓

Read the Annual Report

↓

Identify Key Value Drivers

↓

Forecast Free Cash Flow

↓

Estimate WACC

↓

Calculate Terminal Value

↓

Estimate Enterprise Value

↓

Calculate Equity Value

↓

Interpret Results

This reflects the structured approach used by investment banking, equity research, corporate development, and private equity professionals.

docs.title: Mental Model
docs.text:
Think of a DCF as bringing future cash back to today.

Future Cash

↓

Discount for Time and Risk

↓

Present Value

↓

Intrinsic Value

A DCF is not about predicting the future perfectly.

It is about estimating what a business is worth based on reasonable assumptions and understanding how those assumptions influence value.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• A DCF estimates the intrinsic value of a business.
  • Valuation is based on future Free Cash Flow.
  • Future cash flows must be discounted because of the time value of money.
  • WACC represents the required return of capital providers.
  • Terminal Value captures value beyond the forecast period.
  • DCF valuation is highly dependent on assumptions.
  • AI can accelerate valuation workflows but cannot replace professional judgement.

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Using the Breville FY2025 Annual Report, identify the assumptions you would need before performing a DCF valuation.

Consider:

  • Revenue growth
  • Operating margins
  • Capital expenditure
  • Working capital
  • Tax rates
  • Long-term growth prospects
  • Business risks that may affect the discount rate

Do not calculate the valuation yet.

Focus on understanding which business drivers determine value.

Goal: Appreciate that a DCF begins with understanding the business, not entering formulas into Excel.

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text: Continue to Learn LBO Mechanics, where you will explore how private equity investors evaluate acquisitions using leverage, debt repayment, and investor returns.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Financial models are the foundation of modern finance.

Whether you work in investment banking, equity research, private equity, corporate finance, FP&A, or consulting, financial models help transform historical financial information into forward-looking insights that support better decision-making.

A financial model is much more than an Excel spreadsheet.

It is a structured representation of how a business operates.

This lesson introduces the mechanics behind financial modelling and explains how professional models are designed before learning how to build one in Excel.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
After completing this lesson, you should be able to:

  • Understand the purpose of financial modelling.
  • Recognise the components of a professional financial model.
  • Explain how the three financial statements interact.
  • Understand how assumptions drive forecasts.
  • Appreciate why model design is just as important as model calculations.

docs.title: What is a Financial Model?
docs.text:
A financial model is a simplified representation of a business that helps answer financial questions.

Models allow finance professionals to estimate future performance by combining historical data with reasonable assumptions.

Instead of asking:

"What happened?"

Financial models help answer:

  • What is likely to happen?
  • Why might it happen?
  • What happens if assumptions change?
  • What is the business worth?
  • Should we invest?

Financial modelling transforms historical information into forward-looking decision support.

docs.title: Why Do Finance Professionals Build Models?
docs.text:
Financial models support decisions across many areas of finance.

Examples include:

  • Valuing a company
  • Forecasting revenue
  • Estimating cash flow
  • Budgeting
  • Capital allocation
  • Investment decisions
  • Acquisitions
  • Debt financing
  • Scenario analysis

Although different industries use different models, the underlying principles remain consistent.

docs.title: A Financial Model Begins With a Business
docs.text:
Before opening Excel, understand the business.

Ask questions such as:

  • How does the company make money?
  • What drives revenue?
  • What are the major costs?
  • Is the business seasonal?
  • What risks affect performance?
  • Which metrics matter most?

A model should reflect how the business actually operates.

Understanding the business always comes before building the spreadsheet.

docs.title: The Three Financial Statements
docs.text: Professional financial models are built around three interconnected financial statements.

docs.title: Income Statement
docs.text:
Measures profitability over a period.

Key outputs include:

  • Revenue
  • Gross Profit
  • Operating Profit
  • Net Income

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Represents the company's financial position at a point in time.

It includes:

  • Assets
  • Liabilities
  • Equity

The Balance Sheet must always satisfy:

Assets = Liabilities + Equity

docs.title: Cash Flow Statement
docs.text:
Tracks how cash moves through the business.

Cash flows are classified into:

  • Operating Activities
  • Investing Activities
  • Financing Activities

Cash is ultimately what sustains a business.

docs.title: Everything Is Connected
docs.text:
The three financial statements are not independent.

A change in one statement often affects the others.

For example:

Increase Revenue

↓

Higher Net Income

↓

Higher Retained Earnings

↓

Higher Equity

↓

Higher Cash (if collected)

Understanding these relationships is one of the most important skills in financial modelling.

docs.title: Assumptions Drive Every Model
docs.text:
Financial models are built on assumptions.

Examples include:

  • Revenue growth
  • Gross margin
  • Operating expenses
  • Capital expenditure
  • Working capital
  • Tax rates
  • Interest rates

These assumptions determine future financial performance.

Changing assumptions changes the entire model.

A model is only as credible as the assumptions that support it.

docs.title: Inputs, Calculations and Outputs
docs.text: Professional financial models separate information into three layers.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
Information entered by the user.

Examples:

  • Historical financials
  • Growth assumptions
  • Margin assumptions
  • Discount rate

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text:
The logic that transforms inputs into forecasts.

Examples include:

  • Revenue forecasts
  • Depreciation schedules
  • Debt schedules
  • Cash flow calculations

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
The information decision-makers use.

Examples include:

  • Financial statements
  • Valuation
  • Charts
  • Ratios
  • Investment recommendations

Separating inputs, calculations and outputs improves transparency and reduces errors.

docs.title: Good Models Are Easy to Understand
docs.text:
Professional models should be:

  • Logical
  • Consistent
  • Transparent
  • Flexible
  • Easy to audit

Complexity does not make a better model.

Clarity does.

A model should be understandable by someone other than its creator.

docs.title: The Importance of Model Integrity
docs.text:
Professional financial models must be reliable.

Good models include:

  • Balance checks
  • Error flags
  • Consistency checks
  • Clear assumptions
  • Documented calculations

Building confidence in the model is just as important as producing outputs.

docs.title: Financial Modelling is an Iterative Process
docs.text:
Financial modelling is rarely linear.

Professionals continuously refine assumptions as new information becomes available.

The process typically follows this cycle.

Understand the Business

↓

Collect Historical Data

↓

Build Assumptions

↓

Forecast Financial Statements

↓

Validate Results

↓

Refine Assumptions

↓

Repeat

Models improve through iteration.

docs.title: Where AI Fits Into Financial Modelling
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence does not replace financial modelling.

Instead, it enhances the modelling process.

AI can assist with:

  • Summarising annual reports
  • Identifying business drivers
  • Generating forecasting assumptions
  • Explaining accounting concepts
  • Reviewing formulas
  • Detecting inconsistencies
  • Drafting investment memos

However, finance professionals remain responsible for validating assumptions, checking calculations and exercising professional judgement.

AI accelerates modelling.

It does not replace critical thinking.

docs.title: Financial Modelling in the AI Finance Lab
docs.text:
Throughout the AI Finance Lab, financial modelling will follow the same structured workflow.

Understand the Business

↓

Read the Annual Report

↓

Analyse Historical Financial Statements

↓

Develop Assumptions

↓

Build the Financial Model

↓

Value the Business

↓

Interpret the Results

↓

Communicate Insights

This mirrors the workflow used by finance professionals in investment banking, private equity, equity research and corporate finance.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Financial models represent how businesses operate.
  • Models transform historical data into forward-looking insights.
  • Every model is driven by assumptions.
  • The Income Statement, Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Statement are interconnected.
  • Good models are transparent, flexible and easy to audit.
  • AI enhances financial modelling but does not replace professional judgement.

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Before creating the financial model, study the annual report and answer:

  • How does Breville make money?
  • What are its key revenue drivers?
  • Which costs appear most significant?
  • What factors could influence future growth?
  • Which financial metrics deserve the most attention?

Only after understanding the business should you explore the mechanics of the completed three-statement model.

Goal: Learn to model the business—not just the spreadsheet.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Continue to Learn DCF Mechanics, where you will explore how future cash flows are converted into an estimate of enterprise value using the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation methodology.

docs.title: Understanding How Private Equity Investors Evaluate Acquisitions
docs.text:
A Leveraged Buyout (LBO) is a transaction where an investor acquires a company using a combination of:

  • Equity capital
  • Debt financing

The fundamental question an LBO analysis answers is:

Can an investor acquire a company, improve its performance, reduce debt, and generate an attractive return?

LBO models are widely used by:

  • Private Equity firms
  • Investment banks
  • Corporate development teams
  • Financial sponsors

docs.title: The Core Idea Behind an LBO
docs.text:
In a traditional acquisition, investors primarily rely on business value creation.

In an LBO, investors use financial leverage to enhance returns.

The basic concept:

Purchase Company
        ↓
Use Debt + Equity Financing
        ↓
Improve Business Performance
        ↓
Generate Cash Flow
        ↓
Repay Debt
        ↓
Sell Company
        ↓
Generate Investor Returns

docs.title: Why Use Debt?
docs.text:
Debt can increase investor returns because investors contribute less equity capital upfront.

Example:

docs.title: Acquisition Without Debt
docs.text:
Company Purchase Price:

$100 million

Investor Equity:

$100 million

docs.title: Acquisition With Debt
docs.text:
Company Purchase Price:

$100 million

Debt:

$60 million

Investor Equity:

$40 million

The investor controls the same business with less initial capital.

However, debt also increases risk because the company must make interest and principal payments.

docs.title: The Main Components of an LBO Model
docs.text:
An LBO model typically includes:

  1. Transaction Assumptions
  2. Sources and Uses
  3. Operating Forecast
  4. Debt Schedule
  5. Investor Returns Analysis

docs.title: 1. Transaction Assumptions
docs.text:
The first step is defining the acquisition terms.

Key assumptions include:

docs.title: Purchase Price
docs.text:
The amount paid to acquire the company.

Usually expressed as an:

Enterprise Value

or

EV / EBITDA multiple

Example:

Purchase Price = 10.0x EBITDA

docs.title: Entry Valuation
docs.text:
Investors evaluate whether the acquisition price is reasonable.

Common metrics:

  • Enterprise Value
  • EBITDA
  • EV / EBITDA multiple
  • Revenue multiple

docs.title: Financing Structure
docs.text: The transaction is funded through:

docs.title: Debt
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Term loans
  • Senior debt
  • Subordinated debt

docs.title: Equity
docs.text: Capital contributed by investors.

docs.title: 2. Sources and Uses
docs.text:
The Sources and Uses section answers:

Where does the money come from, and where does it go?

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Money required for the transaction:

  • Purchase of equity
  • Refinancing existing debt
  • Transaction fees
  • Advisory costs

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
Funding available:

  • New debt
  • Investor equity

The fundamental relationship:

Total Sources = Total Uses

A balanced sources and uses table is a critical LBO modelling check.

docs.title: 3. Operating Forecast
docs.text:
The LBO depends on the company's ability to generate cash.

The model forecasts:

docs.title: Revenue Growth
docs.text:
Questions:

  • Can the company grow sales?
  • Are growth assumptions realistic?

docs.title: EBITDA Margin
docs.text:
Questions:

  • Can profitability improve?
  • Are there operational efficiencies?

docs.title: Cash Flow Generation
docs.text:
Cash flow determines how quickly debt can be repaid.

Key drivers:

  • EBITDA
  • Taxes
  • Capital expenditure
  • Working capital changes

docs.title: 4. Debt Schedule
docs.text:
Debt repayment is one of the most important parts of an LBO.

The debt schedule tracks:

  • Beginning debt balance
  • New borrowing
  • Mandatory repayments
  • Optional repayments
  • Interest expense
  • Ending debt balance

docs.title: Debt Paydown
docs.text: A successful LBO typically creates value through:

docs.title: Cash Flow Generation
docs.text:
The business generates cash.

↓

docs.title: Debt Reduction
docs.text:
Cash is used to repay debt.

↓

docs.title: Increased Equity Value
docs.text: Lower debt increases the value attributable to shareholders.

docs.title: 5. Exit Analysis
docs.text:
After holding the investment for several years, investors sell the company.

The model calculates:

docs.title: Exit Enterprise Value
docs.text:
Usually based on:

Exit EBITDA × Exit Multiple

docs.title: Equity Value
docs.text:
The value remaining after paying debt.

Equity Value =
Enterprise Value - Debt

docs.title: Measuring Investor Returns
docs.text: The two primary LBO return metrics are:

docs.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
docs.text:
Measures the annualised return generated by the investment.

It considers:

  • Initial investment
  • Holding period
  • Final proceeds

docs.title: Multiple of Money (MoM)
docs.text:
Measures how many times the original investment has grown.

Formula:

MoM =
Exit Equity Value /
Initial Equity Investment

Example:

An investment grows from:

$100 million

to

$250 million

MoM:

2.5x

docs.title: Sources of LBO Returns
docs.text: Private equity investors typically create value through three sources:

docs.title: 1. EBITDA Growth
docs.text:
The company becomes more profitable.

Example:

EBITDA increases from $50m to $80m.

docs.title: 2. Multiple Expansion
docs.text:
The market values the company at a higher multiple.

Example:

Entry:

8.0x EBITDA

Exit:

10.0x EBITDA

docs.title: 3. Debt Paydown
docs.text: The company reduces debt using generated cash flow.

docs.title: LBO Risks
docs.text: LBOs can create attractive returns, but they also introduce risks.

docs.title: Excessive Leverage
docs.text: Too much debt can limit financial flexibility.

docs.title: Poor Operating Performance
docs.text: If EBITDA declines, debt repayment becomes difficult.

docs.title: Multiple Compression
docs.text: A lower exit valuation multiple can reduce investor returns.

docs.title: Interest Rate Risk
docs.text: Higher borrowing costs can reduce cash flow.

docs.title: LBO Modelling and AI
docs.text:
AI can assist with:

  • Building initial model structures
  • Reviewing formulas
  • Explaining debt schedules
  • Analysing sensitivity cases
  • Documenting assumptions

However, AI cannot decide:

  • Whether leverage is appropriate
  • Whether management assumptions are realistic
  • Whether the acquisition strategy makes sense

Those decisions require investment judgement.

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this module, you should understand:

  • What an LBO transaction is
  • Why leverage affects returns
  • How an LBO model is structured
  • How investors generate returns
  • Key risks in leveraged acquisitions

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Imagine you are part of a private equity investment team considering the acquisition of Breville.

Using only the annual report, evaluate:

  • Does the business generate stable cash flow?
  • Is revenue predictable?
  • What opportunities exist to improve profitability?
  • Could the company support additional debt?
  • What risks would concern a financial sponsor?

Prepare a short investment memo summarising your findings.

Goal: Learn to differentiate the thinking of a private equity investor from a public market analyst.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Continue your learning journey:

  1. LBO Modelling
  2. Merger Modelling
  3. AI-Assisted Financial Modelling

A strong LBO analyst understands not only the spreadsheet mechanics, but also the investment logic behind every assumption.

docs.title: Understanding How M&A Transactions Create (or Destroy) Value
docs.text:
A merger model evaluates the financial impact of combining two companies.

The central question of a merger model is:

Does this transaction create value for shareholders?

Merger models are commonly used by:

  • Investment banks
  • Corporate development teams
  • Private equity firms
  • Strategic acquirers

They help decision-makers understand how an acquisition affects:

  • Revenue
  • Earnings
  • Capital structure
  • Ownership
  • Shareholder value

docs.title: What Is a Merger Model?
docs.text: A merger model combines the financial information of two companies:

docs.title: Acquirer
docs.text: The company purchasing another business.

docs.title: Target
docs.text:
The company being acquired.

The model analyses the combined company after the transaction.

A typical merger model includes:

Acquirer Standalone
        +
Target Standalone
        +
Transaction Adjustments
        ↓
Combined Company
        ↓
Accretion / Dilution Analysis

docs.title: Why Build a Merger Model?
docs.text: Companies pursue acquisitions for many reasons:

docs.title: Revenue Synergies
docs.text:
The combined company generates additional sales.

Examples:

  • Cross-selling products
  • Entering new markets
  • Expanding customer base

docs.title: Cost Synergies
docs.text:
The combined company reduces expenses.

Examples:

  • Removing duplicate functions
  • Improving purchasing power
  • Combining operations

docs.title: Strategic Benefits
docs.text:
Acquisitions may provide:

  • New technology
  • Market access
  • Talent
  • Competitive advantages

docs.title: The Merger Modelling Workflow
docs.text:
A professional merger model generally follows these steps:

Transaction Assumptions
        ↓
Standalone Company Analysis
        ↓
Sources & Uses
        ↓
Purchase Accounting
        ↓
Combined Financial Statements
        ↓
Accretion / Dilution Analysis

docs.title: Step 1: Transaction Assumptions
docs.text:
The model begins with understanding the deal structure.

Key assumptions include:

docs.title: Purchase Price
docs.text:
The value paid for the target company.

Common measures:

  • Enterprise Value
  • Equity Value
  • Premium paid to target shareholders

docs.title: Financing Method
docs.text: Transactions may be funded through:

docs.title: Cash
docs.text: The acquirer pays using available cash.

docs.title: Debt
docs.text: The acquirer borrows funds.

docs.title: Stock
docs.text: The acquirer issues shares to target shareholders.

docs.title: Combination
docs.text: Many transactions use multiple financing methods.

docs.title: Step 2: Standalone Company Analysis
docs.text:
Before combining companies, analyse each business separately.

For both companies, review:

docs.title: Income Statement
docs.text:
Including:

  • Revenue
  • EBITDA
  • Operating income
  • Net income

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Including:

  • Assets
  • Liabilities
  • Debt
  • Equity

docs.title: Key Metrics
docs.text:
Including:

  • Margins
  • Growth rates
  • Earnings per share

Understanding standalone performance is essential before analysing transaction impact.

docs.title: Step 3: Sources and Uses
docs.text: Similar to LBO modelling, merger models require a sources and uses analysis.

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Where money goes:

  • Purchase equity value
  • Refinancing target debt
  • Transaction fees
  • Advisory costs

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
Where funding comes from:

  • Cash
  • Debt
  • Stock issuance

The model must balance:

Total Sources = Total Uses

docs.title: Step 4: Purchase Accounting
docs.text:
When one company acquires another, accounting adjustments are required.

The model may include:

docs.title: Elimination of Target Equity
docs.text: The target's historical equity is removed.

docs.title: Creation of Goodwill
docs.text:
Goodwill represents the premium paid above identifiable net assets.

Conceptually:

Purchase Price
-
Fair Value of Net Assets
=
Goodwill

docs.title: Asset Write-Ups
docs.text:
Certain assets may be revalued during acquisition accounting.

Examples:

  • Property
  • Intangible assets
  • Technology

docs.title: Step 5: Combining Financial Statements
docs.text:
The combined company is created by adding:

Acquirer Financials
+
Target Financials
+
Transaction Adjustments
=
Combined Company

Adjustments may include:

  • New debt
  • Interest expense
  • Synergies
  • Depreciation impacts
  • Goodwill

docs.title: Step 6: Synergy Analysis
docs.text: Synergies are one of the most important deal assumptions.

docs.title: Revenue Synergies
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Increased sales
  • Expanded customer relationships

docs.title: Cost Synergies
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Lower operating expenses
  • Reduced overhead

A good model separates:

  • Realistic synergies
  • Aggressive assumptions
  • Downside scenarios

docs.title: Step 7: Accretion / Dilution Analysis
docs.text: The key output of a merger model is whether the transaction increases or decreases earnings per share.

docs.title: Accretive Transaction
docs.text:
The acquisition increases earnings per share.

Example:

Standalone EPS:

$2.00

Combined EPS:

$2.20

The transaction is accretive.

docs.title: Dilutive Transaction
docs.text:
The acquisition decreases earnings per share.

Example:

Standalone EPS:

$2.00

Combined EPS:

$1.80

The transaction is dilutive.

docs.title: Key Merger Model Outputs
docs.text: A completed merger model typically shows:

docs.title: Transaction Summary
docs.text:
• Purchase price
  • Premium paid
  • Financing structure

docs.title: Financial Impact
docs.text:
• Revenue impact
  • EBITDA impact
  • Net income impact

docs.title: Ownership Analysis
docs.text: Shows ownership percentages after the transaction.

docs.title: Accretion / Dilution
docs.text: Shows impact on shareholder earnings.

docs.title: Unrealistic Synergies
docs.text: Assuming benefits that are difficult to achieve.

docs.title: Ignoring Financing Costs
docs.text: Debt financing creates additional interest expense.

docs.title: Incorrect Purchase Accounting
docs.text: Failing to correctly account for goodwill and adjustments.

docs.title: Overlooking Integration Risks
docs.text: A model may show attractive numbers, but execution determines success.

docs.title: AI-Assisted Merger Modelling
docs.text: AI can support merger analysis by helping with:

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
• Summarising company information
  • Analysing strategic rationale
  • Reviewing industry trends

docs.title: Financial Analysis
docs.text:
• Comparing companies
  • Identifying synergies
  • Creating transaction summaries

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Drafting deal summaries
  • Explaining assumptions
  • Creating investment committee materials

However, AI cannot determine:

  • Whether the acquisition strategy makes sense
  • Whether management can execute integration
  • Whether the purchase price is justified

Those decisions require judgement.

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this module, you should understand:

  • Why companies pursue mergers and acquisitions
  • The roles of acquirers and targets
  • How transactions are structured
  • How purchase price and financing affect outcomes
  • How synergies influence deal value
  • How accretion/dilution analysis measures transaction impact

docs.title: Try It Yourself — Breville Case Study
docs.text:
Open the Breville FY2025 Annual Report included in this repository under the Case Studies > Breville Group section.

Imagine De'Longhi, another global consumer products company, is considering acquiring Breville.

Using the Breville annual report, identify:

  • Strategic reasons for an acquisition.
  • Potential revenue synergies.
  • Potential cost synergies.
  • Integration challenges.
  • Risks that could prevent value creation.

Prepare a memo discussing whether such an acquisition would make strategic sense.

Goal: Learn to evaluate acquisitions from both a strategic and financial perspective.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: After understanding merger mechanics, continue to:

docs.title: Merger Modelling (Coming Soon)
docs.text:
You will learn how to build a complete merger model including:

  • Transaction assumptions
  • Sources and uses
  • Purchase accounting adjustments
  • Financing impacts
  • Combined financial statements
  • Accretion / dilution analysis
  • Sensitivity analysis

A strong merger modeller understands both the spreadsheet mechanics and the strategic logic behind the transaction.

docs.title: Turning Business Stories into Numbers
docs.text:
Financial modelling is one of the most valuable skills in modern finance.

At its core, a financial model is simply a structured representation of a business built in Excel (or another spreadsheet tool). It helps finance professionals understand how a company operates today and how it may perform in the future.

Whether you're valuing a company, evaluating an acquisition, preparing a budget, or analysing an investment, financial modelling provides a framework for making informed decisions.

docs.title: Why Financial Modelling Matters
docs.text:
Finance professionals build models to answer questions such as:

  • How much is a company worth?
  • What happens if revenue grows by 10%?
  • Can the business support additional debt?
  • Should the company acquire a competitor?
  • How sensitive are profits to changes in costs?
  • What return might investors expect?

Rather than relying on intuition alone, financial models allow decisions to be tested using data and assumptions.

docs.title: What Is a Financial Model?
docs.text:
A financial model combines:

  • Historical financial information
  • Business assumptions
  • Mathematical relationships
  • Financial logic

to estimate future business performance.

Most models forecast:

  • Revenue
  • Costs
  • Profitability
  • Cash Flow
  • Balance Sheet
  • Financing
  • Company valuation

A model is only as good as the assumptions that drive it.

docs.title: The Building Blocks of Every Model
docs.text: Most financial models follow a similar structure.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
These are assumptions controlled by the modeller.

Examples include:

  • Revenue growth
  • Gross margin
  • Tax rate
  • Interest rate
  • Capital expenditure
  • Working capital assumptions

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text:
This is where business logic is applied.

Examples:

  • Revenue forecasts
  • Depreciation schedules
  • Debt schedules
  • Interest calculations
  • Cash flow projections

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Outputs communicate the results of the model.

Common outputs include:

  • Income Statement
  • Balance Sheet
  • Cash Flow Statement
  • Valuation
  • Charts
  • Dashboards
  • Scenario comparisons

docs.title: Types of Financial Models
docs.text: Different business questions require different models.

docs.title: Three-Statement Model
docs.text:
The foundation of corporate finance.

Links together:

  • Income Statement
  • Balance Sheet
  • Cash Flow Statement

docs.title: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)
docs.text:
Used to estimate the intrinsic value of a business based on expected future cash flows.

Commonly used in:

  • Investment Banking
  • Equity Research
  • Private Equity
  • Corporate Development

docs.title: Leveraged Buyout (LBO)
docs.text:
Evaluates whether an acquisition financed with debt can generate attractive investor returns.

Typically used by:

  • Private Equity firms

docs.title: Merger Model
docs.text:
Assesses the financial impact of combining two companies.

Often referred to as:

  • Accretion/Dilution Analysis

Used in:

  • Mergers & Acquisitions
  • Corporate Development

docs.title: Characteristics of a Good Financial Model
docs.text: Professional financial models should be:

docs.title: Transparent
docs.text: Users should easily understand the logic.

docs.title: Flexible
docs.text: Assumptions should be easy to modify.

docs.title: Accurate
docs.text: Calculations should be reliable and internally consistent.

docs.title: Auditable
docs.text: Errors should be easy to identify.

docs.title: Well Formatted
docs.text: Professional formatting improves readability and reduces mistakes.

docs.title: Common Modelling Best Practices
docs.text:
Experienced financial modellers typically follow several principles:

  • Separate inputs, calculations, and outputs.
  • Avoid hardcoding values inside formulas.
  • Use consistent formatting.
  • Build models in logical sections.
  • Include error checks.
  • Document key assumptions.
  • Keep formulas as simple as possible.

Good models are designed to be maintained—not just built.

docs.title: Where AI Fits into Financial Modelling
docs.text:
Artificial Intelligence is changing how finance professionals build and review models.

AI can assist with:

  • Formula generation
  • Error detection
  • Documentation
  • Data extraction
  • Financial statement analysis
  • Spreadsheet automation

However, AI cannot replace:

  • Business judgement
  • Financial reasoning
  • Assumption selection
  • Understanding company strategy

The strongest finance professionals will use AI as a productivity tool rather than a substitute for financial expertise.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text: This section of AI Finance Lab will progressively cover:

docs.title: Foundations
docs.text:
• Excel modelling principles
  • Model architecture
  • Best practices
  • Formatting standards

docs.title: Core Modelling Skills
docs.text:
• Three-statement modelling
  • Forecasting
  • Scenario analysis
  • Sensitivity analysis

docs.title: Advanced Models
docs.text:
• DCF valuation
  • Leveraged Buyout (LBO)
  • Merger models
  • Integrated financial models

docs.title: AI-Assisted Modelling
docs.text:
Learn how AI can help you:

  • Build faster
  • Review models
  • Identify inconsistencies
  • Automate repetitive tasks
  • Improve documentation

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
We recommend completing the modelling modules in the following order:

  1. Prompt Engineering
  2. Model Auditing
  3. Model Balancing
  4. Model Formatting
  5. Financial Modelling Mechanics
  6. DCF Mechanics
  7. LBO Mechanics
  8. Merger Mechanics
  9. Financial Modelling
  10. DCF Modelling
  11. LBO Modelling
  12. Merger Modelling

Each lesson builds on the previous one, gradually developing the skills used by investment bankers, corporate finance professionals, private equity investors, and financial analysts.

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text:
A financial model is not just an Excel spreadsheet.

It is a structured representation of how a business creates value.

Learning to build robust financial models develops analytical thinking, improves business understanding, and provides a foundation for applying AI effectively in finance.

In the chapters ahead, you'll learn not only how to build financial models, but also why they are built that way.
